The real thing to be careful about is not making the payment arrangement so easy that again you wind up having people as clients who you shouldn't have. See, what you never want is the person who is going down for the third time and looking to you for the life raft. Every once in a while you might actually be able to save one, but that ain't good business. That's good if you're running a charity or you're an evangelist or something, you should be doing that, but not in business because the odds aren't good. Most of the time they're going to be dragging you into the undertow with them. So if they're so weak that they can't pay for whatever it is that you're offering them with some reasonable accommodation, so let's say a $25,000 thing being sold to a dentist. Well, he might not want to lay $25,000 on his AMX all at one time and we can kind of understand that and he might not want to write a check for $25,000 all at one time. But what would be too little? Well, $1,000 a month for 24 months. You know, if he can't pay it off faster than that, you probably don't want. And in many cases what we're doing with people, they need to be able to invest some money to implement. So if they have to sell their paraplegic mother-in-law's wheelchair and mortgage the furniture and stuff in order just to get in, how are they going to do anything? Now you might occasionally make an exception. And I have done it over the years. If somebody is really determined is in that situation and comes begging and convinces you that they are going to apply themselves and they, you know, now I'd never let them in for nothing and I would never discount, but I might play with their payment arrangements. But as a published or offered to everybody kind of thing, you've got to find a point where not so demanding that nobody will give it to you or nobody could give it to you out of your clientele and market, but not so easy to pay for that anybody can, including people you shouldn't have in there in the first place. Exclusivity. Well the most powerful lever you will ever have in selling coaching or consulting, either one, is again fewer chairs. Right? Well the fewest number of chairs you can ever put in front of a group of people is one because you can't sell no chairs. So the fewest number of chairs you can ever put in front of people is one. That's why exclusivity works is because there's only one chair. There's all kinds of exclusivity. There's one per business. So I'm only going to let one chiropractor in. I'm only going to let one dentist in. I'm only going to let one podiatrist in. I'm only going to let one retail store owner in. There's those kinds of exclusivity. There's geographic exclusivity. I'm only going to take one person from each zip code town, however you define an area, and that does answer an objection by the way all the time to well what happens if everybody in my town is using your stuff? Well that objection is gone now because there's only one person in the town using your stuff. There's another way to answer that objection too which is how I used to answer. But the exclusivity makes it go away. The exclusivity can be by industry, it could be by size of business, it could be by geography, any which way you cut it, as soon as you create it and you make that part of what you're selling, you gain an enormous amount of leverage because now you're holding up one stool and there's only one stool. And if you don't get it, the guy across the street is going to get it and you're going to see him sitting in the stool and now you're really going to be unhappy. The other reason it works is an extension of that. As soon as you start to sell exclusive things, there's a buy it because they want it and there's a buy it because they don't want that person to have it factor. And that is often as big a driving force as is the I want it. My example about all that which I'll do as briefly as I can is Larry King's story. But it'll stick it in your mind forever so it's worth the retelling. Larry tells a story about how when he was on the radio in Miami, he was a radio host in Miami. He gets a call and it's an obvious bent nose. It's somebody of Italian descent who explains that they're hosting a tribute dinner for the local Monsignor and it would be ever so nice if Larry would emcee it as a favor. And these are people you don't decline favors to. So Larry goes to host the tribute. He arrives, they explained him in the back, there's a few things you ought to know. One don't ask anybody to raise the lights. We're keeping the lights dim because there's some competitors in the room who don't generally like each other. There's some gentlemen from the FBI and so we're just going to keep the lights dim.
